exam_overview.calculator_policy: A graphing calculator is required for Section I Part B and Section II Part A. No calculator is permitted for Section I Part A or Section II Part B. Students must memorize all formulas and identities — no formula sheet is provided.
frq_structure: FRQ 2: Modeling a Non-Periodic Context (calculator allowed, Units 1–2, real-world scenario)
FRQ 3: Modeling a Periodic Context (no calculator, Unit 3, real-world scenario)
tips: No formula sheet is provided — memorize key identities including Pythagorean, sum, and double-angle formulas.
Unit 4 (Parameters, Vectors, and Matrices) is NOT assessed on the AP exam; focus study time on Units 1–3.
The no-calculator section emphasizes algebraic manipulation, exact trig values, and symbolic reasoning — practice these without a calculator.
The calculator section emphasizes graphical interpretation, regression, and numerical approximations — know how to use your graphing calculator efficiently.
Each FRQ is worth 6 points; show all work clearly since partial credit is awarded.

units.name: Polynomial and Rational Functions
key_topics: Covariation and how input/output values change together
Average rate of change and secant line interpretation
Concavity: rates of change increasing (concave up) vs. decreasing (concave down)
Polynomial end behavior determined by leading term degree and sign
Zeros and multiplicity: even multiplicity bounces, odd multiplicity crosses
Complex zeros come in conjugate pairs (a + bi and a - bi)
Even functions (f(-x) = f(x)) and odd functions (f(-x) = -f(x))
Rational function end behavior: horizontal and slant asymptotes
Vertical asymptotes vs. holes (removable discontinuities) in rational functions
Transformations: vertical/horizontal translations and dilations
Polynomial long division and equivalent representations
Binomial theorem using Pascal's Triangle

key_formulas.name: Average Rate of Change
key_formulas.note: Slope of the secant line; for linear functions this is constant, for quadratics the AROC changes at a constant rate

key_formulas.name: General Polynomial Form
key_formulas.note: End behavior is governed entirely by the leading term a_n x^n

key_formulas.name: Function Transformations
key_formulas.note: Vertical dilation |a| (reflect if a < 0), horizontal compression 1/|b| (reflect if b < 0), shift right h, shift up k

key_formulas.name: Polynomial Division
key_formulas.note: Used to find slant asymptotes and rewrite rational expressions

key_formulas.name: Binomial Theorem
key_formulas.note: Coefficients come from Pascal's Triangle row n

learning_objectives: Describe how input and output values of a function vary together (covariation) and represent this graphically
Determine and interpret average rates of change over intervals; identify concavity from rate of change patterns
Identify key characteristics of polynomial functions including zeros (with multiplicity), end behavior, and even/odd symmetry
Determine zeros, vertical asymptotes, horizontal asymptotes, and holes of rational functions
Rewrite polynomial and rational expressions in equivalent forms using long division and the binomial theorem
Construct and apply function transformations (translations and dilations) to build new function models
common_question_types: Given a graph or table, identify the average rate of change over an interval and describe how it changes across the domain
Determine the zeros (including complex) and end behavior of a polynomial function from its equation or graph
Identify vertical asymptotes, holes, and horizontal asymptotes of a rational function from its factored form
Apply a transformation to a parent function and match or construct the resulting graph or equation
exam_tips: When a rational function has the same factor in numerator and denominator, it creates a hole, NOT a vertical asymptote — the factor must appear more times in the denominator than numerator for a vertical asymptote.
For end behavior of rational functions: equal degrees give y = (leading coeff ratio); numerator degree one higher gives a slant asymptote found via polynomial long division.
Remember that a degree-n polynomial has exactly n complex zeros counting multiplicity — if you find fewer real zeros, the rest are complex conjugate pairs.

units.name: Exponential and Logarithmic Functions
key_topics: Arithmetic sequences (constant difference) vs. geometric sequences (constant ratio)
Linear functions as continuous analogs of arithmetic sequences; exponential as geometric
Exponential function f(x) = ab^x: initial value, base, growth vs. decay
Exponential properties: product rule b^m * b^n = b^(m+n), power rule (b^m)^n = b^mn
Horizontal translation of exponential equivalent to vertical dilation
Composition of functions (f ∘ g)(x) = f(g(x))
Inverse functions: domain and range swap; f^(-1) undoes f
Logarithm as the inverse of an exponential: log_b(x) = y iff b^y = x
Logarithm properties: product, quotient, and power rules
Change of base formula for calculator evaluation
Solving exponential and logarithmic equations algebraically
Semi-log plots: exponential data appears linear when y-axis is log-scaled

key_formulas.name: Geometric Sequence
key_formulas.note: Common ratio r; contrast with arithmetic sequence a_n = a_0 + nd

key_formulas.name: General Exponential Function
key_formulas.note: a = initial value = f(0); b > 1 for growth, 0 < b < 1 for decay

key_formulas.name: Continuous Growth Model
key_formulas.note: r > 0 for growth, r < 0 for decay; e ≈ 2.718

key_formulas.name: Logarithm Definition
key_formulas.note: Always ask: 'b to what power gives x?' to evaluate logarithms

key_formulas.name: Logarithm Properties
key_formulas.formula: log_b(MN) = log_b M + log_b N  |  log_b(M/N) = log_b M - log_b N  |  log_b(M^p) = p * log_b M
key_formulas.note: Used to expand or condense log expressions and to solve exponential equations

key_formulas.name: Change of Base Formula
key_formulas.note: Converts any logarithm to natural log for calculator evaluation

learning_objectives: Identify and construct exponential functions from context, tables, or graphs; distinguish growth from decay
Apply exponent and logarithm properties to rewrite expressions in equivalent forms
Determine the inverse of an exponential function and recognize that logarithms are inverses of exponentials
Solve exponential and logarithmic equations and inequalities using properties and inverse operations
Construct and validate function models (exponential vs. linear vs. other) given data or context
Interpret semi-log plots to determine whether data follows an exponential model and construct the linearized form
common_question_types: Given a table of values with a constant ratio, construct and apply an exponential model to predict future values
Solve an equation of the form a * b^x = c by applying logarithms, or solve log_b(x) = c by converting to exponential form
Use logarithm properties to rewrite a complex log expression as a single logarithm or expand it into a sum/difference
Interpret a semi-log plot or composition of functions in context and determine properties of the underlying model
exam_tips: When solving exponential equations, always isolate the exponential expression before taking the log of both sides — taking log of a sum is not the same as a sum of logs.
Remember that f(x + k) = b^(x+k) = b^k * b^x — a horizontal shift of an exponential function is equivalent to multiplying by a constant (vertical dilation).
Always check solutions to logarithmic equations for extraneous solutions: the argument of any logarithm must be strictly positive.

units.name: Trigonometric and Polar Functions
key_topics: Periodic functions: period, amplitude, midline, and phase shift
Radian measure and arc length on the unit circle
Unit circle: sin(θ) = y-coordinate, cos(θ) = x-coordinate, tan(θ) = sin/cos
Exact values at key angles: 0, π/6, π/4, π/3, π/2 and their quadrant extensions
Sinusoidal function f(θ) = a*sin(b(θ + c)) + d and its parameters
Tangent function: period π, asymptotes at π/2 + kπ
Reciprocal trig functions: sec, csc, cot and their key characteristics
Pythagorean identities and derived forms
Sum and double-angle identities for sine and cosine
Inverse trig functions and their restricted domains
Solving trigonometric equations and inequalities
Polar coordinates (r, θ) and conversion between rectangular and polar forms
Polar function graphs and average rate of change of polar functions

key_formulas.name: Sinusoidal Function
key_formulas.note: Amplitude = |a|, Period = 2π/|b|, Phase shift = -c, Midline = y = d

key_formulas.name: Pythagorean Identities
key_formulas.formula: sin²θ + cos²θ = 1  |  tan²θ + 1 = sec²θ  |  1 + cot²θ = csc²θ
key_formulas.note: The first identity is fundamental; the others follow by dividing by cos²θ or sin²θ

key_formulas.name: Sum Identities
key_formulas.formula: sin(α + β) = sinα cosβ + cosα sinβ  |  cos(α + β) = cosα cosβ - sinα sinβ
key_formulas.note: Must be memorized — no formula sheet provided

key_formulas.name: Double-Angle Formulas
key_formulas.note: Derived from sum identities with α = β = θ

key_formulas.name: Polar-Rectangular Conversion
key_formulas.formula: x = r cosθ,  y = r sinθ  |  r = sqrt(x² + y²),  θ = arctan(y/x) [adjusted for quadrant]
key_formulas.note: Convert between forms as needed; adjust arctan result based on the quadrant of (x, y)

key_formulas.name: Reciprocal Trig Functions
key_formulas.formula: sec θ = 1/cos θ  |  csc θ = 1/sin θ  |  cot θ = cos θ/sin θ
key_formulas.note: Undefined wherever the denominator function equals zero

learning_objectives: Determine sine, cosine, and tangent values using the unit circle including exact values at key angles
Identify and interpret the amplitude, period, phase shift, and midline of sinusoidal functions from equations and graphs
Construct sinusoidal models for periodic real-world phenomena and answer questions about their behavior
Rewrite trigonometric expressions using Pythagorean, sum, and double-angle identities
Solve trigonometric equations and inequalities, accounting for the periodic nature of trig functions
Convert between rectangular and polar coordinates and construct graphs of polar functions
common_question_types: Identify or construct a sinusoidal function from a graph or contextual description by determining amplitude, period, midline, and phase shift
Use a Pythagorean or sum identity to rewrite a trig expression in a simplified or equivalent form
Solve a trigonometric equation on a given interval, finding all solutions using the unit circle or inverse trig functions
Convert a point or equation between rectangular and polar form, or interpret the graph of a polar function r = f(θ)
exam_tips: When reading a sinusoidal graph, the amplitude is half the distance from max to min (NOT the max value itself), and the midline is the average of max and min.
Inverse trig function restricted domains must be memorized: arcsin returns values in [-π/2, π/2], arccos in [0, π], arctan in (-π/2, π/2). These restrictions ensure uniqueness.
For polar function rate-of-change problems, treat r as the output and θ as the input — the average rate of change is Δr/Δθ, interpreted as how fast the radius changes per radian.
